did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning and computer vision techniques, specifically the EfficientNet architecture (EfficientNet-B0 to EfficientNet-B7), to classify three types of skin cancer (basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and melanoma) using the ISIC 2019 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the design, training, and evaluation of EfficientNet models (B0-B7) for skin cancer classification using the ISIC 2019 dataset. It includes specific sections on Method (data acquisition, preprocessing, model architecture), Results (training/validation metrics, testing accuracy/precision/recall/F1-score), and Discussion, which are characteristic of a primary research study rather than a review.

title: Skin cancer classification using EfficientNet architecture
journal: Bulletin of Electrical Engineering and Informatics
brief_summary: This study addresses the classification of three major skin cancer types—basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and melanoma—using the EfficientNet architecture. The researchers utilized the ISIC 2019 dataset, preprocessing the images by resizing them to 256×256 pixels and handling class imbalance through undersampling. They trained and compared eight EfficientNet models, ranging from EfficientNet-B0 to EfficientNet-B7, to identify the most effective architecture for this task. The evaluation focused on accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score metrics derived from the test results. The EfficientNet-B4 model achieved the highest performance with 79.69% accuracy, 81.67% precision, 76.56% recall, and 79.03% F1-score. The findings confirm that EfficientNet architectures are capable of effectively classifying these common skin cancers, with EfficientNet-B4 being the optimal choice among those tested.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin cancer (Basal cell carcinoma, Squamous cell carcinoma, and Melanoma)
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of three specific skin cancer types (BCC, SCC, MEL) using the EfficientNet architecture on the ISIC 2019 dataset. The model predicts discrete class labels, evaluated via accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019 Dataset (via Kaggle)
datasets_used.description: The original dataset was sourced from the ISIC 2019 challenge, provided by Larxel on Kaggle, and retransformed by Maharana for easier use. The study utilized a subset of this dataset containing images of skin cancer.

use.training: Used for training EfficientNet-B0 to EfficientNet-B7 models (70% of the balanced dataset).
use.validation: Used for validation during training to monitor loss and accuracy (20% of the balanced dataset).
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of model performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) (10% of the balanced dataset).
use.other: The original dataset contained 25,331 images across 8 classes, but only 3 classes were selected. Undersampling was applied to balance the classes, reducing the total count from the original subset to 1,893 images.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) and loss values without statistical inference.

ISIC 2019 Dataset (Balanced Subset).notes: The dataset was preprocessed by resizing images to 256x256 pixels and applying undersampling to balance the three classes (BCC, SCC, MEL). The original subset had 25,331 images across 8 classes; the study used 1,893 images (631 per class). Training and validation were conducted with early stopping (patience=10) and a maximum of 150 epochs. Testing was performed on the held-out test set (192 images).
ISIC 2019 Dataset (Balanced Subset).summary_notes: EfficientNet-B4 achieved the highest test accuracy (79.69%). EfficientNet-B5 achieved the highest training (78.46%) and validation (75.93%) accuracy. EfficientNet-B3 achieved the highest test precision (85.98%) and F1-score (79.21%). All models were evaluated on the same test set of 192 images.

architecture_details.transfer_learning: All layers from each EfficientNet model were frozen to prevent pre-trained weights from being modified
modifications: Top layer replaced with Global Average Pooling (GAP) layer
Dropout layer added to prevent overfitting

preprocessing_techniques: Class selection: Filtered the original 8-class ISIC 2019 dataset to include only three classes: Basal cell carcinoma (BCC), Squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), and Melanoma (MEL).
Undersampling: Applied to handle class imbalance by selecting 631 samples from each of the three classes, resulting in a balanced dataset of 1,893 images.
Image Resizing: Resized all images to 256×256 pixels to ensure consistency and reduce computational resources.
Image Augmentation: Applied random flip and random rotation to the training images to increase diversity and reduce overfitting.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to training images to increase diversity and reduce overfitting.
Applied to training images to increase diversity and reduce overfitting.

conclusions: All tested EfficientNet models (B0 to B7) are capable of effectively classifying basal cell carcinoma (BCC), squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), and melanoma, demonstrating good accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.
The EfficientNet-B4 architecture achieved the highest performance among the tested models, with 79.69% accuracy, 81.67% precision, 76.56% recall, and 79.03% F1-score.
EfficientNet-B3 achieved the highest precision (85.98%) and F1-score (79.21%), while EfficientNet-B5 achieved the highest training (78.46%) and validation (75.93%) accuracy.
The study confirms that the EfficientNet architecture is a suitable and effective method for skin cancer classification tasks.
Misclassifications occurred primarily between BCC and SCC, indicating potential challenges in distinguishing these specific non-melanoma skin cancers.
The EfficientNet-B4 model is identified as the optimal choice for skin cancer classification among the EfficientNet variants tested in this study.
important_remarks: The study uses a significantly undersampled subset of the ISIC 2019 dataset (1,893 images) to balance classes, which limits the generalizability of the results compared to training on the full dataset.
The testing set is very small (192 images total, 64 per class), which may lead to high variance in performance metrics and reduces statistical power.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on the held-out test set from the same source dataset.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was reported to validate the differences in performance between the EfficientNet variants.

The study focuses only on three classes (BCC, SCC, Melanoma), excluding other common skin lesions (e.g., seborrheic keratosis, nevi) present in the original dataset, which limits clinical applicability for differential diagnosis.
Misclassifications were noted primarily between BCC and SCC, indicating a specific challenge in distinguishing these non-melanoma skin cancers.
Image preprocessing included resizing to 256x256 pixels, which the authors acknowledge may eliminate some important features, potentially impacting performance.
The study does not report sensitivity/specificity trade-offs or ROC-AUC curves, relying solely on accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.
Data leakage risks are not explicitly addressed, though standard train/validation/test splits were used.
